Description: Abstracts data access behind an interface so business logic is decoupled from the database layer
When To Use: When you need testable data access; when switching between Eloquent/Doctrine; for complex queries
When Not To Use: Simple CRUD with few models; when Eloquent scopes suffice
Anti-Pattern: Calling Eloquent directly in controllers: User::where('email', $email)->first() scattered everywhere

Description: Encapsulates business logic in dedicated service classes keeping controllers thin
When To Use: When business logic spans multiple models; when the same logic is reused across controllers
When Not To Use: When a controller action is simple CRUD with no complex logic
Anti-Pattern: Fat controllers with hundreds of lines of business logic mixed with HTTP concerns

Description: A simple object to transfer data between layers without behavior. Ensures type safety and explicit contracts
When To Use: When passing data between layers; for API request/response shaping; for form data
When Not To Use: When a simple array is clear enough; for trivial internal operations
Anti-Pattern: Passing raw arrays between layers: $data = $request->all(); $service->create($data);

Description: Creates objects without specifying exact classes. Centralizes complex object creation logic
When To Use: When object creation is complex; when the type depends on runtime conditions
When Not To Use: When a simple new ClassName() suffices
Anti-Pattern: $message);"
Complexity: Giant switch/if-else chains in business logic to decide which class to instantiate

Description: Define a family of algorithms as implementations of an interface and swap them at runtime
When To Use: When behavior needs to be swappable (payment gateways, export formats, auth strategies)
When Not To Use: When there is only one implementation
Anti-Pattern: Hardcoding payment logic with if/else for different gateways in one method

Description: Components subscribe to events and react when they are dispatched. Decouples producers from consumers
When To Use: For side effects (notifications, logging, cache invalidation); when multiple systems react to one action
When Not To Use: When there is only one reaction; when the flow must be synchronous and ordered
Anti-Pattern: Calling notification/email/cache code directly inside the controller after order creation

Description: Wraps request/response handling in layers that can inspect/modify at each stage
When To Use: For cross-cutting concerns: auth, logging, CORS, rate limiting, locale detection
When Not To Use: When logic is specific to a single route handler
Anti-Pattern: Putting auth/validation checks at the top of every single controller method

Description: Wraps an existing implementation to add behavior without modifying original code
When To Use: For adding caching, logging, retry logic around existing services
When Not To Use: When the original class can simply be modified; when wrapping adds no real value
Performance Impact: Mixing caching logic directly inside repository queries

Description: Constructs complex objects step by step using a fluent interface
When To Use: When object construction has many steps; for query building or configuration objects
When Not To Use: When a simple constructor with a few params suffices
Anti-Pattern: Building complex queries with many conditionals inline in the controller

Description: Ensures only one instance of a class exists. In PHP most singletons are managed by the DI container
When To Use: When exactly one instance must exist (config manager, logger); via the service container
When Not To Use: When you need multiple instances; when it makes testing harder

Description: Provides a static-like interface to classes registered in the service container
When To Use: For convenient access to services; when you want clean readable code in blades/views
When Not To Use: In service classes where DI is preferred; when it hides too many dependencies
Anti-Pattern: Accessing global variables or static methods without any abstraction layer

Description: Single-purpose invokable classes that encapsulate one specific business operation
When To Use: When a service method grows complex; for discrete business operations that can be tested independently
When Not To Use: When logic is trivial enough for a controller method
Anti-Pattern: Bloated service classes with dozens of methods and responsibilities

Description: Encapsulates business rules as reusable specification objects that can be combined
When To Use: For complex filtering where rules are reused across the app; for combining conditions with AND/OR
When Not To Use: Simple one-off where clauses; when Eloquent scopes cover the need
Anti-Pattern: Duplicating the same where clauses across multiple controllers and services

Description: Passes data through a series of processing stages where each stage transforms the result
When To Use: For multi-step data processing; for request validation chains; for ETL-like operations
When Not To Use: When processing is a single simple step
Anti-Pattern: Deeply nested function calls or sequential if/else blocks for multi-step processing

Description: Converts one interface to another to make incompatible types work together
When To Use: When integrating third-party SDKs; when wrapping legacy code with your interface
When Not To Use: When types already satisfy the required interface
Anti-Pattern: Calling third-party SDK methods directly throughout your codebase

Description: Immutable objects that represent domain concepts and are compared by value not identity
When To Use: For domain concepts like Money, Email, Address; when validation at creation ensures correctness
When Not To Use: When a primitive type is clear enough; for entities that need identity
Anti-Pattern: strpos($this->value
Performance Impact: Using raw strings for email/money/phone throughout the app without validation

Description: Laravel Form Requests encapsulate validation and authorization logic per request type
When To Use: Always for Laravel projects — separates validation from controllers; for complex validation rules
When Not To Use: For simple one-field validations in API endpoints
Anti-Pattern: Validating manually in the controller with if/else: if (empty($request->title)) { return error; }

Description: Transforms models into API-friendly JSON responses with controlled field exposure
When To Use: For API responses where model structure differs from desired output; for hiding internal fields
When Not To Use: When the model structure exactly matches the desired output
Anti-Pattern: Returning $user->toArray() or $user->toJson() directly exposing all database columns

Description: Centralizes authorization logic per model into policy classes
When To Use: When authorization rules are model-specific; for role-based access control
When Not To Use: For simple boolean checks not tied to a model
Anti-Pattern: Checking permissions inline in every controller: if ($user->id !== $post->user_id) { abort(403); }

Description: PHP 8.1+ backed enums for type-safe status/type values with built-in validation
When To Use: For status fields (order status, user role); for any fixed set of values
When Not To Use: When values are truly dynamic and come from a database table
Anti-Pattern: Using string constants or plain strings: const STATUS_PENDING = 'pending'; without type safety

Description: Eloquent local and global scopes encapsulate reusable query constraints
When To Use: For frequently used query filters; for status-based filtering; for multi-tenant queries
When Not To Use: When a query is used only once in the entire codebase
Anti-Pattern: Repeating ->where('status', 'published')->whereNotNull('published_at') in every query

Description: Separates command (what to do) from handler (how to do it) for clear intent and testability
When To Use: For CQRS; when actions need to be queued/async; for complex business operations
When Not To Use: Simple CRUD that doesn't need command/handler separation
Anti-Pattern: Putting all creation logic directly in the controller with no separation of concern

Description: PHP traits for composing reusable behavior across unrelated classes
When To Use: For shared behavior that doesn't fit inheritance (HasUuid, Sluggable, SoftDeletes)
When Not To Use: When inheritance is more appropriate; when a trait does too much (God trait)
Anti-Pattern: Duplicating UUID generation logic in every model's boot method

Description: Provides a default no-op implementation instead of returning null to avoid null checks
When To Use: When null checks proliferate through the code; for optional dependencies
When Not To Use: When null has meaningful semantics that should be handled differently
Anti-Pattern: if ($discount !== null) { $total -= $discount->calculate($subtotal); } else { $total = $subtotal; }

Description: Registering implementations in the service container for automatic dependency injection
When To Use: For swapping implementations; for configuring complex object graphs; for testing with mocks
When Not To Use: When a class has no interface and no complex construction
Anti-Pattern: Using new StripeGateway() everywhere instead of resolving from container

Description: An entity that serves as the entry point for a cluster of domain objects maintaining consistency
When To Use: For complex domains with invariants; when multiple entities must change together
When Not To Use: Simple CRUD; when business rules are minimal
Anti-Pattern: Modifying order items directly from outside the Order without going through the aggregate

Description: Define roles with hierarchy and check permissions in middleware. Use spatie/laravel-permission or similar
When To Use: When you need fine-grained access control; for admin panels; for multi-tenant apps
When Not To Use: For simple binary auth; for public APIs without user roles
Anti-Pattern: Checking role strings in every controller: if ($user->role !== 'admin') { abort(403); }

Description: Listens to Eloquent model lifecycle events (creating
When To Use: updating
When Not To Use: deleting) for side effects
Code Example: For audit logging; for cache invalidation on model changes; for sending notifications on state change
Anti-Pattern: When the side effect is a one-time thing; when event dispatch is more appropriate
Performance Impact: Adding audit/cache/notification logic in every save() call across the app

Description: Dispatches time-consuming tasks to a background queue for async processing
When To Use: For sending emails; for processing uploads; for any I/O-heavy task that shouldn't block the response
When Not To Use: For instant lightweight operations; when the user needs an immediate result
Anti-Pattern: Processing images synchronously in the controller making the user wait 10+ seconds

Description: Treats individual objects and compositions of objects uniformly through a tree structure
When To Use: For menu systems; for permission hierarchies; for category trees
When Not To Use: When a flat structure suffices; when recursion adds complexity without benefit
Anti-Pattern: Building nested menus with deeply nested arrays and manual recursive rendering logic
